rationale_for_baseline_tier: A benign symmetrical, afebrile, non-erythematous inflammatory/mechanical flare of the small hand joints is not a medical emergency and is appropriately managed with telehealth symptomatic care (NSAID, warm soaks, rest, reassurance) plus a routine GP review within about a week for confirmation, rheumatology referral and a long-term plan (T1). Telephone triage escalated it for urgent assessment specifically to EXCLUDE septic polyarthritis and manage acute functional loss — once the septic features screen negative, the correct disposition is routine, not emergency. The clinical work here is avoiding an unnecessary ED presentation while keeping a clear septic-joint safety net. Under uncertainty the safety net (ESC-001/ESC-002) escalates conservatively to ED.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint suddenly becomes intensely red, hot to touch and severely painful to move even slightly
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the emergency department the same day, without delay, if this develops
escalation_edges.rationale: A monoarticular hot, red, exquisitely painful joint must be treated as possible septic arthritis — a joint emergency needing urgent ED assessment and joint aspiration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any single joint suddenly turns bright red, feels hot, and is so painful you can barely move it, go straight to the emergency department that same day — that pattern needs urgent assessment to rule out a joint infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A high fever with chills develops alongside the joint symptoms
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend the emergency department promptly if fever/chills develop
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with acute arthritis raises the probability of joint infection or a systemic process and warrants urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you spike a high fever or get the shivers along with the joint pain, please go to the emergency department promptly — fever with hot joints needs to be checked urgently.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The pain spreads to large joints (knees, shoulders), OR the swelling persists unchanged for more than a week despite anti-inflammatories
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange a prompt GP review within 24-48 hours (or sooner than the routine 7-day appointment) if this occurs
escalation_edges.rationale: Spread or persistence beyond a self-limited flare suggests a more active inflammatory arthritis needing earlier reassessment and possible disease-modifying therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain starts spreading to bigger joints like your knees or shoulders, or the swelling hasn't budged after a week on the anti-inflammatories, book in to see your GP sooner rather than waiting.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is struggling with grip and self-care tonight during the acute flare
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Offer practical support advice for tonight (analgesia timing, warm soaks, adaptive tips, keeping a phone within reach) and check she can manage safely; a low threshold to involve a support person or after-hours nurse line if she cannot cope, without escalating the clinical tier.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult with a background of chronic intermittent inflammatory joint disease
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm no contraindication to an ongoing NSAID course (GI, renal, cardiovascular risk) at the GP review; not resolvable via telehealth.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of joint inflammation brought on by overusing your hands, not a joint infection or permanent damage. Treat it at home with the anti-inflammatory taken with food, warm-water soaks and resting your hands, and see your GP within about a week to confirm things and plan longer-term care. Watch for the warning signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP appointment within about 7 days; use telehealth re-contact or your GP sooner if the warning signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a warning sign develops after hours — a single hot red intensely painful joint, or a high fever with chills — go to the emergency department; for spreading or persistent swelling, contact an after-hours GP service or your GP the next day.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth note documenting a symmetrical, afebrile, non-erythematous small-joint flare on a background of chronic intermittent finger arthralgia; septic features screened negative; NSAID + conservative measures advised; GP to confirm on examination, check inflammatory markers and consider rheumatology referral and a long-term NSAID/DMARD plan.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with NO septic-arthritis safety net and no GP follow-up — so an evolving single hot/red joint or new fever would not be acted upon.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A joint that later becomes truly infected is missed, leading to cartilage destruction, permanent joint damage or systemic sepsis.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring without arranging any GP follow-up or clear return advice, leaving a potentially progressive inflammatory arthritis unmonitored.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a classic benign symmetrical, afebrile inflammatory flare to urgent care, the emergency department or an ambulance — the exact inappropriate emergency presentation the consult was intended to prevent, causing avoidable cost, patient distress and system load.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Septic arthritis and a benign inflammatory/mechanical flare can overlap early, and the confirmatory findings (joint warmth/fluctuance, passive micro-motion pain, vitals, serology) cannot be obtained via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage as a benign flare on the balance of the negative red-flag screen WHILE explicitly stating the septic-joint safety net and arranging GP review — hold the presumptive label openly rather than committing to a confirmed diagnosis remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either collapsing the uncertainty into false certainty and dropping the safety net (dismissive reassurance), or collapsing it the other way into an unnecessary emergency referral for a benign flare.
